description: Sandblasting, also known as abrasive blasting, uses compressed air to propel abrasive materials like sand, grit, or beads at high speeds to remove paint, rust, or contaminants from surfaces.
key_differences: Unlike non-abrasive methods like laser or dry ice blasting, sandblasting is highly abrasive, causing significant surface damage and generating substantial dust, requiring extensive containment and cleanup; it's cheaper than laser but less eco-friendly than dustless or soda blasting due to higher waste and potential for substrate erosion.
all_up_cost_explanation: High total cost due to extensive setup and cleanup requirements. Setup: $275, Consumables: $55/hr. Additional cleanup costs ($200-$500/job) and disposal fees ($100-$300) increase total by 40-60% due to CA air quality regulations.
surface_damage: High: Abrasive action causes pitting, warping, or erosion on softer/delicate materials; compromises substrate integrity.
extra_setup_cleanup_factors: Amplified by regs (e.g., dust containment): Cleanup $200–$500/job; disposal $100–$300. Increases total by 40–60%.
notes: Higher due to urban logistics and CA air quality rules; not ideal for precision tech applications.
cleaning_rate_explanation: Moderate cleaning rate (140 sq ft/hr) limited by extensive dust containment requirements and need for careful surface preparation in urban environments.
cost_per_sq_ft_explanation: Higher per-square-foot cost due to significant cleanup expenses, disposal fees, and CA regulatory compliance requirements for dust containment.
avg_rate_explanation: Adjusted hourly rate includes markup for urban logistics, compliance with CA air quality rules, and extensive cleanup requirements. Not cost-effective for precision applications.

description: Dustless blasting combines abrasive media with water in a high-pressure system to strip surfaces, suppressing dust by up to 95% compared to traditional dry blasting.
key_differences: Compared to dry sandblasting, it reduces dust and airborne particles significantly, making it safer and more compliant with regulations; however, it still poses moderate surface damage unlike zero-damage methods like laser or dry ice, and requires water management not needed in soda or laser cleaning.
all_up_cost_explanation: Highest all-up cost driven by water reclamation requirements and premium environmental compliance. Setup: $250, Consumables: $80/hr. Water management adds $50-$100 per job, with total 15-35% uplift for compliance.
surface_damage: Moderate: Reduced abrasion due to water suppression, but risks damage on sensitive or thin surfaces; better than dry but not zero-impact.
extra_setup_cleanup_factors: Water reclamation adds $50–$100; still 15–35% uplift for compliance/mess.
notes: Preferred for environmental regs; mobile ops common in Bay Area.
cleaning_rate_explanation: Slower cleaning rate (120 sq ft/hr) due to water management requirements and need for careful operation to balance dust suppression with surface protection.
cost_per_sq_ft_explanation: Highest per-square-foot cost due to specialized equipment, water reclamation requirements, and environmental compliance overhead. Premium for eco-friendly mobile operations.
avg_rate_explanation: Premium hourly rate reflects Bay Area environmental compliance standards, water reclamation costs, and specialized mobile operation logistics. Preferred for projects requiring dust suppression.

description: Laser cleaning employs focused laser beams to vaporize contaminants, coatings, or rust from surfaces through ablation without physical contact or chemicals.
key_differences: It offers precision and no surface damage, differing from abrasive methods like sand or soda blasting that can etch materials; unlike dry ice or dustless blasting, it produces no waste or media residue, but is slower and more expensive upfront than blasting techniques.
all_up_cost_explanation: Most cost-effective for precision work with zero additional costs. Setup: $0, Consumables: $0/hr. No cleanup fees, disposal costs, or compliance overhead makes it cheaper overall despite higher base rate.
surface_damage: Minimal/None: Non-contact process vaporizes contaminants without abrading or altering the substrate; ideal for delicate materials.
extra_setup_cleanup_factors: Low impact: Setup $0/job; no disposal fees. No additional costs beyond hourly rate.
notes: Premium for innovation demand; e.g., $250–$350 common in CA. Minimal extras make it cost-effective overall for clean jobs.
cleaning_rate_explanation: Slower cleaning rate (80 sq ft/hr) offset by precision and zero cleanup time. No setup or teardown delays make actual project time competitive with faster methods.
cost_per_sq_ft_explanation: Mid-range per-square-foot cost with no hidden fees. No cleanup, disposal, or compliance costs mean the displayed rate is the true final cost.
avg_rate_explanation: Flat hourly rate with no adjustments or hidden fees. Premium reflects precision capability and zero cleanup requirements. Most cost-effective for delicate substrates and tech applications.

description: Dry ice blasting propels pellets of frozen carbon dioxide at high velocity; upon impact, the pellets sublimate, creating a thermal shock that lifts contaminants without residue.
key_differences: It leaves no secondary waste like sand or soda blasting, and causes no abrasion unlike dustless or sand methods; faster than laser for large areas but requires dry ice supply chain, which isn't needed in laser cleaning, and can be noisier than other non-abrasive options.
all_up_cost_explanation: High cost driven by dry ice supply chain and consumables. Setup: $550, Consumables: $150/hr. Despite fast cleaning, dry ice logistics and storage requirements add significant overhead.
surface_damage: Minimal: Non-abrasive; pellets sublimate on impact, no residue or substrate wear.
extra_setup_cleanup_factors: Low: Minimal waste; setup $100–$200; compliance adds minor costs. Increases total by 10–15%.
notes: High demand in tech sectors; supply chain costs elevate rates in CA.
cleaning_rate_explanation: Fastest cleaning rate (500 sq ft/hr) ideal for large-area applications. Speed advantages offset higher consumable costs for high-volume projects.
cost_per_sq_ft_explanation: Lowest per-square-foot cost due to exceptional speed. Best value for large surface areas where dry ice supply chain is readily available.
avg_rate_explanation: Moderate hourly rate elevated by Silicon Valley dry ice supply chain costs. Popular in tech sectors for food-grade cleaning and electronics applications requiring zero residue.

description: Soda blasting uses sodium bicarbonate (baking soda) particles propelled by compressed air to gently remove contaminants, often used for delicate surfaces.
key_differences: Gentler than sandblasting with less damage, but still abrasive unlike laser or dry ice; produces soluble waste that's easier to clean than sand but requires pH neutralization not needed in dustless blasting, and is faster for soft contaminants compared to laser.
all_up_cost_explanation: Lowest all-up cost but requires moderate cleanup. Setup: $275, Consumables: $90/hr. CA waste disposal regulations add $150-$350 cleanup costs, increasing total by 30-40%.
surface_damage: Moderate: Mild abrasive; gentler than sand but can etch soft surfaces if overused.
extra_setup_cleanup_factors: Moderate: Waste disposal under CA regs; cleanup $150–$350. Increases total by 30–40%.
notes: Eco regs favor it; used for graffiti in urban areas like San Carlos.
cleaning_rate_explanation: Fast cleaning rate (450 sq ft/hr) effective for graffiti removal and soft contaminants. Speed and gentler action make it popular for urban restoration projects.
cost_per_sq_ft_explanation: Second-lowest per-square-foot cost. Efficient for delicate substrates where speed matters more than zero-damage requirements. Eco-friendly waste disposal adds minor costs.
avg_rate_explanation: Competitive hourly rate favored by CA environmental regulations. Popular for urban graffiti removal and historical restoration where gentler abrasion is acceptable.
